Title: Writing Compilers in Python (with PLY) - Speaker Deck
URL: https://speakerdeck.com/dabeaz/writing-compilers-in-python-with-ply
Content: #### Writing Compilers in Python (with PLY). Avatar for David Beazley   David Beazley. Avatar for David Beazley dabeaz. : expr PLUS term -> p\_expr(p) (3) | expr MINUS term (4) | term (5) term : term TIMES factor -> p\_term(p) (6) | term DIVIDE factor (7) | factor (8) factor : NUMBER -> p\_factor(p) Grammar PLY Rules Action: LR Example: Step 1 stack input X = 3 + 4 \* 5 $end. NAME ‘X’ shift = 3 + 4 \* 5 $end (1) assign : NAME EQUALS expr -> p\_assign(p) (2) expr : expr PLUS term -> p\_expr(p) (3) | expr MINUS term (4) | term (5) term : term TIMES factor -> p\_term(p) (6) | term DIVIDE factor (7) | factor (8) factor : NUMBER -> p\_factor(p). PLY Rules LR Example: Step 2 stack input Action: (1) assign : NAME EQUALS expr -> p\_assign(p) (2) expr : expr PLUS term -> p\_expr(p) (3) | expr MINUS term (4) | term (5) term : term TIMES factor -> p\_term(p) (6) | term DIVIDE factor (7) | factor (8) factor : NUMBER -> p\_factor(p).

Title: python 3.x - How to get the grammar production when there is an error with Ply(Yacc)? - Stack Overflow
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/64536801/how-to-get-the-grammar-production-when-there-is-an-error-with-plyyacc
Content: ### more stack exchange communities. # How to get the grammar production when there is an error with Ply(Yacc)? In the `yacc.py` file I defined the output of a grammar and also an error, like this:. In addition to this error message, I also want to print what was the production read at the time of the error, something like:. You particularly can't with a bottom-up parser like the one generated by Ply, but even with top-down parsers "the production read at the time" is not a very well-defined concept. So which production is the parser trying to complete when it sees the token `return`? In a top-down parser, it would be possible to walk up the parser stack to get a list of partially-completed productions in inclusion order. So, for example, when it sees a `)` following `if (x < y`, it immediately knows that it must finish up the `expression` production and proceed with the rest of the `if` statement.

Title: PLY (Python Lex-Yacc) — ply 4.0 documentation
URL: https://ply.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ply.html
Content: # PLY (Python Lex-Yacc)¶. This document provides an overview of lexing and parsing with PLY. If you’re using an older version of Python, you’re out of luck. PLY is a pure-Python implementation of the compiler construction tools lex and yacc. The main goal of PLY is to stay fairly faithful to the way in which traditional lex/yacc tools work. Thus, if you’ve used yacc in another programming language, it should be relatively straightforward to use PLY. Since PLY was primarily developed as an instructional tool, you will find it to be fairly picky about token and grammar rule specification. The rest of this document assumes that you are somewhat familiar with parsing theory, syntax directed translation, and the use of compiler construction tools such as lex and yacc in other programming languages. The `lex.py` module is used to break input text into a collection of tokens specified by a collection of regular expression rules.

Title: PLY (Python Lex-Yacc) - Computer Science
URL: https://www.cs.vu.nl/~jansa/ftp/BK0/ply.html
Content: # PLY (Python Lex-Yacc). PLY is a Python-only implementation of the popular compiler construction tools lex and yacc. However, the overall flavor of the implementation is more closely modeled after the C version of lex and yacc. The other significant feature of PLY is that it provides extensive input validation and error reporting--much more so than other Python parsing tools. Because PLY was primarily developed as an instructional tool, you will find it to be *MUCH* more picky about token and grammar rule specification than most other Python parsing tools. yacc.py is used to recognize language syntax that has been specified in the form of a context free grammar.

Title: [PDF] Writing Parsers and Compilers with PLY - David Beazley
URL: https://www.dabeaz.com/ply/PLYTalk.pdf
Content: Breakdown • Parse ﬁle with 1000 random expressions (805KB) and build an abstract syntax tree • Total time : 2.95 sec • Startup : 0.02 sec • Lexing : 1.20 sec • Parsing : 1.12 sec • AST : 0.61 sec • System: MacPro 2.66Ghz Xeon, Python-2.5 Advanced PLY • PLY has many advanced features • Lexers/parsers can be deﬁned as classes • Support for multiple lexers and parsers • Support for optimized mode (python -O) Class Example import ply.yacc as yacc class MyParser: def p_assign(self,p): ‘’’assign : NAME EQUALS expr’’’ def p_expr(self,p): ‘’’expr : expr PLUS term | expr MINUS term | term’’’ def p_term(self,p): ‘’’term : term TIMES factor | term DIVIDE factor | factor’’’ def p_factor(self,p): ‘’’factor : NUMBER’’’ def build(self): self.parser = yacc.yacc(object=self) Experience with PLY • In 2001, I taught a compilers course • Students wrote a full compiler • Lexing, parsing, type checking, code generation • Procedures, nested scopes, and type inference • Produced working SPARC assembly code Classroom Results • You can write a real compiler in Python • Students were successful with projects • However, many projects were quite "hacky" • Still unsure about dynamic nature of Python • May be too easy to create a "bad" compiler General PLY Experience • May be very useful for prototyping • PLY's strength is in its diagnostics • Signiﬁcantly faster than most Python parsers • Not sure I'd rewrite gcc in Python just yet • I'm still thinking about SWIG.
